text: The gastrectomy or total gastrectomy is a surgical procedure performed for the treatment of gastric cancer when the tumor is located in the top half or the middle of the stomach. When the tumor is in this location, effective treatment requires that we remove the entire stomach as well as any surrounding lymph nodes. We will then need to reconnect the small intestine to the esophagus. to restore the continuity. The last step of the surgery is to place a feeding tube to temporarily assist with nutrition after surgery.

At TriHealth, we perform this surgery minimally invasively with the use of robotic technology. The following video is of a real total gastrectomy procedure performed at TriHealth. The video has been edited and therefore is significantly shorter than a typical total gastrectomy.
If you are scheduled to have this procedure your surgery will be performed in a similar fashion.
The surgeon lifts the stomach and divides the omentum, a fatty layer connected to the transverse colon. The vessel sealer on the right side of the screen cauterizes and divides the tissue.

The surgeon continues to divide the omentum using a hook cautery, lifting the omentum while pushing down the mesentery of the transverse colon. The omentum is carefully divided, ensuring no major blood vessels are present, as the surgeon works from the upper part of the stomach towards the end, separating the tissue connecting the stomach to the transverse colon.

The surgeon rolls the stomach to access its underside, lifting it to reveal the gastropoploic artery and vein on the left side, which are major blood vessels supplying the stomach. An enlarged lymph node is visible along the blood vessel, which will be removed with the specimen.

The surgeon lifts the stomach to expose the base of the blood vessels diving below the pancreas. The lesser curve of the stomach is visible as the surgeon manipulates the tissue, lifting the stomach and pushing down the pancreas. The hepatic artery is seen running along the top part of the pancreas, supplying blood to the liver. The camera backs up to provide a clear view of the pancreas.

The surgeon lifts the stomach to reveal a firm tumor, then proceeds to separate the stomach from the underlying pancreas by dividing the attachments. The pancreas is pushed down, exposing the splenic artery running along its top part. The surgeon targets the tissue above the splenic artery, where important lymph nodes are located for cancer surgery.

The surgeon observes the tortuous splenic artery twisting on the right side of the screen and lifts tissue off it. The camera backs up to reveal a clear view of the tail of the pancreas. The surgeon continues to divide attachments between the posterior stomach and pancreas, then drops the stomach to focus on the lesser curve. The lesser omentum, a thin veil of tissue connecting the stomach to the liver, is visible above the lesser curve. The liver appears as a maroonish brown organ at the top left of the screen.

The surgeon works along the lesser curve of the stomach, dividing tissue and identifying the right gastric artery. Using a vessel sealer, the artery is cauterized and cut to prevent bleeding. The surgeon then addresses the gastropoploic artery and vein, using a hook to create a window around these vessels for further dissection.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the gastropoploic artery and vein, ensuring the enlarged lymph node is included in the specimen. The stomach is lifted, and umbilical tape is used to position the stapler around the duodenum, allowing for division of the intestine just beyond the stomach.

The surgeon has completely separated the end of the stomach and is now working towards the esophagus. The transverse colon is visible on the right side of the screen, and the surgeon divides tissue close to it, ensuring the fat is included with the stomach specimen. The spleen is visible at the top right, positioned closely to the stomach, and the left cruce of the diaphragm is starting to appear.

The surgeon lifts the stomach off the diaphragm muscle located at the bottom left of the screen, rolling it away from the esophagus. The diaphragm is visible as a large muscle beneath the stomach. The stomach is then flipped to access the opposite side.

The surgeon lifts the stomach to expose the left gastric artery and left coronary vein, surrounded by lymph nodes. The lymph nodes are carefully lifted and separated from the blood vessels, ensuring they are included in the surgical specimen. The hepatic artery is visible, pulsing on the left side, and is preserved while the lymph nodes are peeled off the coronary vein and left gastric artery. The coronary vein, a purple vessel, is isolated and divided using a vessel sealer, which seals and cuts the vessel.

The surgeon exposes and encircles the artery behind the gastropoploic artery and vein, using cauterization to divide it. After dividing the blood vessels, the surgeon lifts the surrounding lymph nodes to include them in the specimen. Below the surgical field, the white tissue of the aorta is visible, and the surgeon carefully lifts tissue off this large blood vessel.

The surgeon approaches the top part of the stomach near the esophagus, identifying and removing a lymph node along the hepatic artery. The tissue at the gastroesophageal (GE) junction is divided, with another lymph node included in the specimen. The diaphragm muscle is pushed down while the stomach and esophagus are lifted up and to the right, separating tissue attached at the GE junction.

The surgeon identifies the diaphragm muscle and proceeds to lift the esophagus, preparing to divide the tissue just below the gastroesophageal (GE) junction. The stomach remains partially attached to prevent retraction into the chest, ensuring better exposure for subsequent steps.

The surgeon places the completely divided specimen, including the stomach and all associated lymph nodes, into a pouch. The hepatic artery lymph node is placed into the bag first, followed by the rest of the specimen. The bag is then closed and prepared for removal to be sent to the pathologist for examination.
With the specimen removed, the surgeon begins the reconstruction phase to restore gastrointestinal continuity, ensuring that ingested food will pass through the intestines properly.

The surgeon lifts the transverse colon to locate the small bowel, identifying the proximal part at the ligament of Treitz. A window is created in the mesentery, a veil of fatty tissue containing blood vessels, to access the small bowel. The small bowel is divided using a stapler, and one end is fed up to the esophagus. Another window is made in the mesentery of the transverse colon to facilitate this connection.

The surgeon passes the small bowel through the mesenteric window and flips the transverse colon down to locate the small bowel. Additional small bowel is pulled through to ensure sufficient length to reach the esophagus. A connection is made between the esophagus and the small bowel by creating holes in both structures and using a stapler to join them.

The surgeon completes the division of the lower esophagus, ensuring the esophagus remains in the abdomen without retracting into the chest. The entire stomach is removed, leaving a hole at the connection between the small bowel and esophagus. The surgeon begins closing this hole with sutures, starting from the right corner of the staple line and sewing towards the center, then repeating the process from the opposite corner.

The surgeon completes the first layer of closure by tying sutures in the middle of the connection between the small bowel and esophagus. A second layer of sutures is then applied, passing through both the small bowel and esophagus, ensuring a secure closure to prevent leaks.

The surgeon creates a connection at the previously divided small bowel by making a hole in each limb and using a stapler to form a common opening. The connection is then reinforced with a two-layered suture closure. The final step involves placing a feeding tube.

The surgeon inserts a J-tube into the jejunum by tacking the small bowel to the abdominal wall. Sutures are passed through the abdominal wall at multiple points to prevent bowel twisting. A hole is made in the small intestine, and the tube is fed through the abdominal wall into the intestine. The tube is secured with sutures to prevent leakage, ensuring it is completely covered. The bowel is visibly moving, indicating readiness for function.
